climate.ocean_source: UK Met Office / UKHO typical monthly values (manual)

showcase.intro_text:
Brighton is a grey-sky beach. That is the first thing to know. The sea is English Channel cold most of the year. The beach is pebble, not sand, and the pebbles hurt your feet. It rains. The pier neon looks best in drizzle. If you arrived here expecting tropical seaside, you misread the postcard — this is a specifically British seaside, and specifically British seaside is its own long, weird, and wonderful thing.

Brighton began as Brighthelmstone, a fishing village on the Sussex coast. In 1754 a physician named Richard Russell published a thesis in Latin arguing that drinking and bathing in seawater cured scrofula, and the medical establishment that followed him effectively invented the modern seaside holiday as a treatment. A few decades later the Prince of Wales, the future George IV, fell in love with the place, built the Royal Pavilion here — an Indo-Islamic pleasure palace with onion domes, chinoiserie interiors, and a kitchen the size of a cathedral — and turned Brighton into the louche English answer to Biarritz. By the time the railway arrived from London in 1841, Brighton was a three-hour train ride from the capital that already had the look it still has: piers, promenades, tall stucco terraces, chalk cliffs off to the east.

Two piers were built after the railway. The West Pier opened in 1866, the Palace Pier in 1899 — both Victorian iron fairy-tales standing in the Channel. The Palace Pier still runs fairground rides and sells doughnuts. The West Pier burned twice in 2003 and 2004, collapsed partially, and its skeleton is still out there in the water — a rusted Victorian ghost that Brighton has chosen to keep rather than dismantle. It is the most photographed ruin in the south of England, and it is what everyone's phone means when they take a Brighton photo.

Alongside the piers, Brighton accumulated a cultural weight disproportionate to its size. Graham Greene set *Brighton Rock* here in 1938 and wrote the most Catholic noir ever — Pinkie Brown and the razor blades under the pier. The Who's 1973 rock opera *Quadrophenia* and its 1979 film retold the 1964 Mods-vs-Rockers bank-holiday fights along Madeira Drive; the bike-chase scenes were shot here. In 1984 the IRA planted a bomb in the Grand Hotel during a Conservative Party conference; Margaret Thatcher survived; five others died. ABBA won Eurovision at the Brighton Dome in 1974 with "Waterloo" — and Europop was changed. In 2002 Fatboy Slim, a Brighton resident under his real name Norman Cook, threw a free beach concert that drew roughly 250,000 people and triggered a police inquiry. Every August the city hosts Pride — the UK's largest — and Kemptown, the gay-village neighbourhood just east of the Palace Pier, is one of Europe's longest-running queer capitals.

Brighton also has weather. In October 1987 the Great Storm — hurricane-force winds that forecasters famously missed — tore through the south coast and ripped the Palace Pier's concert hall into the sea. The storm is a cultural reference point here, the way hurricanes are in Miami. In 2016 the i360 observation tower opened on the seafront beside the West Pier skeleton — a 162-metre British Airways–branded glass pod on a pole that rises and falls. It is not universally loved; it is also unquestionably now part of the skyline.

Below all of this is the beach itself. Pebble and shingle, a shelf dropping into the cold Channel, a promenade raised above the tide line, fishermen working the flats at dawn, kite-surfers where the Hove breakwater thins out. There is no pretending this is a swimming beach in the way Copacabana is. It is, instead, a beach you come to *look at* the sea, or at the West Pier skeleton, or at the piano-cafe that appears on the sand some summers, or at the 1,200-ton Regency terraces that still face the water.

What makes Brighton Brighton is not the sea. It is everything that accumulated on the land behind it over three centuries of people deciding that this specific grey-sky pebble beach was where they would go to be somebody else for a weekend.
showcase.favela_note:
Kemptown is the Brighton neighbourhood just east of the Palace Pier — Regency terraces, steep streets, the Royal Sussex County Hospital, and since the early twentieth century the epicentre of gay Brighton. The first documented Brighton gay pub, The Chequers, opened here in the 1940s. By the 1970s and 80s, Kemptown was one of the few places in the UK where openly gay culture could exist at scale; clubs like **Revenge** and **Legends** became Europe's Saturday-night landmarks. The annual **Brighton & Hove Pride** parade, first held in 1992, is now the UK's largest Pride and draws over 400,000 people across the weekend.

The queer heritage matters for a travel page because it is inseparable from the beach itself. The Kemptown seafront at **Duke's Mound** — a small patch of coast about a mile east of the Palace Pier — was the UK's first legally tolerated naturist beach, designated 1980 after years of informal use. It was also, for most of the twentieth century, one of the few outdoor gay cruising grounds in the country where police arrests did not happen nightly. That history is not a footnote; it is why Brighton became Brighton.

A visitor walking east from the Palace Pier toward Kemptown can read the city's layers in order: the Victorian stucco of the central seafront, the jet-black iron lamp posts of the Kemptown hill, the rainbow flags, the pubs with their pre-war signage, the garden squares hidden behind the streets. If you never walk east of the Palace Pier, you have seen the tourist Brighton — and missed the actual Brighton.

day_in_time.dawn: On a clear morning the Palace Pier's lights are still on as the sky lightens behind it. Fishermen work the shallows off the Hove breakwater. A handful of cold-water swimmers — the Brighton Swimming Club, founded 1860, operates year-round — enter at Banjo Groyne. The promenade is empty except for runners and dog-walkers. The air is usually below 10°C. The West Pier's iron skeleton is at its most dramatic in the first hour of light, silhouetted against the sea haze.
day_in_time.midday: By mid-morning the beach fills with day-trippers from London — the Brighton Line delivers them in waves. Deckchairs appear on the pebbles. Fish and chips are eaten on the Palace Pier. The Lanes and the North Laine behind the seafront are packed with vintage shoppers. In summer temperatures reach 20-22°C and the pebbles get hot enough to burn bare feet. Kite-surfers work the wind off the Hove shore when the south-westerly is up.
day_in_time.golden: Brighton's golden hour is specifically short and specifically magical — the light comes in low off the sea at a hard angle, turns the Regency stucco peach, and catches the iron of the West Pier from the west. Crowds gather at the end of the Palace Pier for sunset; the photograph most people come for happens here. The i360 pod rises slowly. From Kemptown hill, you can see the whole arc of the city lit in amber.
day_in_time.night: The Palace Pier lights up. The Victorian lamps along Madeira Drive come on. Kemptown fills for drinks — Revenge, The Marlborough, The Queen's Arms, the gay capital working at full volume. Under the Palace Pier, teenagers and tourists throw chips to the herring gulls. In summer the music is visible through open doors. In winter the pier is a neon streak in the dark sea and the rest of the seafront is empty by ten.

food_drink.name: Fish and chips on the Palace Pier
food_drink.description: Deep-fried cod, thick-cut chips, salt and malt vinegar, eaten out of a paper tray with a wooden fork while the Palace Pier's neon reflects off your cardboard. Gulls are aggressive. The price is higher than a chip shop inland. It is the point.

food_drink.name: Brighton Rock (the candy)
food_drink.description: A stick of hard red-and-white peppermint sugar with the word 'BRIGHTON' running through its middle like a geological layer. The manufacturing trick (the lettering is laid down hot and pulled) is 19th-century Edwardian craft and still done by hand. Graham Greene's novel takes its title from this candy.
food_drink.where: Any seafront sweet shop; The Candy Brighton on the Palace Pier

food_drink.name: A pint in Kemptown
food_drink.description: The Marlborough, The Queen's Arms, The Stag, or Charles Street — any of the long-running Kemptown LGBTQ+ pubs within ten minutes of the Palace Pier. A local ale and a bench on the pavement. Brighton's best reading of its own history happens at the Marlborough after 8 p.m.

food_drink.name: Fresh fish from English's or Regency
food_drink.description: English's of Brighton (1945, in The Lanes) and the Regency Restaurant (since 1930, on the seafront) are the two old-school fish restaurants that survived the pub-and-burger tide. Dover sole, grilled sea bass, the oyster platter, and tap water in a glass jug.
food_drink.where: English's: East Street, The Lanes · Regency: 131 Kings Road

timeline.title: Brighthelmstone in the Domesday Book
timeline.description: The Norman survey records a fishing village on the Sussex coast paying taxes to William the Conqueror. Brighton's name would not acquire its modern form for another 700 years.

timeline.title: Richard Russell's seawater cure thesis
timeline.description: The Lewes physician Richard Russell publishes a Latin dissertation arguing that drinking and bathing in seawater cures scrofula. The medical fashion that follows effectively invents the modern seaside holiday as a health treatment.

timeline.title: The Prince of Wales's first visit
timeline.description: The future George IV — still the dissolute Prince of Wales — visits Brighton and decides he likes it. Over the next 40 years he transforms a fishing village into the most fashionable English resort.

timeline.title: Royal Pavilion begins
timeline.description: Architect John Nash begins transforming the Prince Regent's Brighton residence into the Royal Pavilion — an Indo-Islamic fantasy with onion domes, chinoiserie interiors, and a cast-iron kitchen. Completed 1822. Still the most unusual royal building in Britain.

timeline.title: London-Brighton railway opens
timeline.description: The London & Brighton Railway inaugurates service, cutting the journey from a day to three hours. Brighton is now a weekend city for the London middle class. The seaside resort as we know it — three-day-trip, not three-week-cure — is born.

timeline.title: West Pier opens
timeline.description: Designed by Eugenius Birch, the West Pier is the most elegant of Britain's Victorian iron piers — a 340-metre promenade with pavilion, concert hall, and landing stage. It closes in 1975 and burns in 2003-2004, but its skeleton is still there.

timeline.title: Volks Electric Railway opens
timeline.description: Magnus Volk opens the world's oldest operating electric railway along Madeira Drive. Still runs. Still has its original 1880s feel. The only seaside electric railway to survive the Victorian era intact.

timeline.title: Palace Pier opens
timeline.description: The Brighton Marine Palace and Pier — universally called the Palace Pier — opens. Ten shillings and sixpence to build. Fairground rides, slot machines, a concert hall at the end. Still working. Still selling doughnuts.

timeline.title: Graham Greene publishes 'Brighton Rock'
timeline.description: Greene's novel — the most Catholic noir ever written, about the razor-gang teenager Pinkie Brown under the pier — makes Brighton's name synonymous with seaside menace for two generations of readers.

timeline.title: Mods vs Rockers, Whitsun bank holiday
timeline.description: On the Whitsun weekend of 1964, Mod scooter gangs and Rocker motorbike gangs clash along Brighton's Madeira Drive and the Aquarium terraces. Photographs of the brawls run worldwide. The incident becomes the defining moment of British youth-culture panic and the backbone of Quadrophenia.

timeline.title: The Who release 'Quadrophenia'
timeline.description: Pete Townshend's rock opera about a Mod called Jimmy ends on Brighton Beach, with the 1964 bank-holiday fights as the final act. The 1979 film version will make the story into British cinema canon.

timeline.title: ABBA win Eurovision at the Brighton Dome
timeline.description: On 6 April 1974, at the Brighton Dome — a Regency concert hall a block from the Pavilion — Sweden's ABBA perform 'Waterloo' and win the Eurovision Song Contest. The moment that makes them global.

timeline.title: UK's first legal naturist beach at Duke's Mound
timeline.description: After years of informal use, Brighton & Hove Council designates a stretch of shoreline near Duke's Mound — about a mile east of the Palace Pier — as the UK's first legally tolerated naturist beach.

timeline.title: IRA bombs the Grand Hotel
timeline.description: In the early hours of 12 October 1984, an IRA bomb planted by Patrick Magee detonates in the Grand Hotel during the Conservative Party conference. Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher survives. Five others die. The bombing reshapes British security culture for a generation.

timeline.title: The Great Storm
timeline.description: On the night of 15–16 October 1987, hurricane-force winds — unpredicted by the BBC weatherman Michael Fish earlier that evening — hit the south coast. Brighton's Palace Pier loses its concert hall to the sea. Eighteen people die nationally. The storm becomes a cultural reference point.

timeline.title: First Brighton Pride
timeline.description: The first Brighton & Hove Pride parade takes place. It will grow to become the UK's largest Pride, drawing 400,000+ across the first weekend of August. The parade ends at Preston Park, a mile inland, but it starts at the beach.

timeline.title: Fatboy Slim's Big Beach Boutique II
timeline.description: On 13 July 2002, Norman Cook (Fatboy Slim) plays a free DJ set on Brighton Beach. An estimated 250,000 people attend — five times the expected crowd. Police close Brighton to incoming visitors for the first time in history. No deaths, but a full public inquiry.

timeline.title: West Pier burns
timeline.description: On 28 March 2003, a fire — never formally solved — destroys the West Pier's concert hall. A second fire follows in May 2003 and a third in December 2004. The pier collapses progressively over the next decade. The iron skeleton that remains becomes Brighton's most photographed landmark.

timeline.title: i360 tower opens
timeline.description: The British Airways i360 observation tower opens on the seafront directly beside the West Pier ruin — a 162-metre steel pole with a glass viewing pod that rises and falls. Designed by Marks Barfield, the team behind the London Eye. Polarising. Unquestionably part of the skyline.

zones.name: Hove beach
zones.character: The western stretch, officially in the city of Hove — which Brighton absorbed only in 1997 but which still insists on its own identity. Regency squares, wider pavements, wealthier. The beach is quieter; the Lawns on the seafront are grass not pebble. Birdwatchers work the Hove Lagoon to the west.
zones.best_for: quiet morning walks, Regency architecture, kite-surfers
zones.notes: Hove insists it is not Brighton. Locals joke about 'Hove, actually.'

zones.name: West Pier & i360
zones.character: The dramatic centre-west of the seafront. The West Pier's burned iron skeleton stands a hundred metres off-shore; the i360 observation tower rises beside it. The Kings Road Arches below the promenade have become the trendy-bar stretch — Jubilee Library to Shoreditch-by-the-sea.
zones.best_for: the best photographs in Brighton, cocktails, the i360
zones.notes: The West Pier Trust runs a small museum in the base of the i360.

zones.name: Central beach (between piers)
zones.character: The postcard stretch. Pebbles, deckchairs, beach volleyball nets in summer, the piano-cafe that sometimes appears on the sand. The Grand Hotel and the Hilton Metropole face the water. This is where day-trippers land and where summer weekends look crowded.
zones.best_for: first-time visits, sunbathing, watching the water
zones.notes: The Grand Hotel facade hides the 1984 IRA bombing's aftermath — rebuilt to original specs.

zones.name: Palace Pier
zones.character: The working pier. Rollercoasters, slot machines, candyfloss, doughnuts, fortune tellers, the horse-race machine. Opened 1899 and still running exactly the same business model. The pier itself is 525 metres long; the views back at the city are the best vertical shots of Brighton.
zones.best_for: fairground rides, the pier walk, proper fish and chips
zones.notes: Free to walk on; you only pay for rides.

zones.name: Madeira Drive
zones.character: The long straight Victorian seafront road east of the Palace Pier, lined with a cast-iron colonnade and the Volks Electric Railway. This is where Mods and Rockers fought in 1964. It is also the start of the London-to-Brighton Veteran Car Run every November.
zones.best_for: architecture walks, Volks Railway, the Whitsun bank-holiday reconstruction
zones.notes: The Madeira Lift (a Grade II-listed 1890 hydraulic lift to the promenade) is being restored.

zones.name: Kemptown & Duke's Mound
zones.character: The eastern stretch. Regency terraces, steep streets, and Brighton's gay village — Kemptown has been the UK's longest-running openly queer neighbourhood. Duke's Mound, a small patch of coast here, was designated the UK's first legal naturist beach in 1980.
zones.best_for: queer Brighton, quieter beach, Regency gardens
zones.notes: Walk east of the Palace Pier or you miss the real Brighton.

landmarks.name: Royal Pavilion
landmarks.description: The Indo-Islamic pleasure palace George IV commissioned — onion domes outside, chinoiserie dragons and palm-tree columns inside, a cast-iron kitchen that was a marvel of its era. Now a museum, run by Brighton & Hove City Council. Nothing else in England looks like it.

landmarks.name: Brighton Palace Pier
landmarks.architect_or_designer: Brighton Marine Palace and Pier Co.
landmarks.description: The working Victorian pleasure pier, 525 metres of iron and timber extending into the Channel. Fairground rides, slot machines, a concert hall at the end. Still selling doughnuts, still free to walk on. The defining image of the Brighton seafront.

landmarks.name: West Pier (ruin)
landmarks.description: Once the most elegant of Britain's Victorian iron piers, closed in 1975 and devastated by fires in 2003 and 2004. The rusted skeleton still standing in the sea is now a protected wildlife roost and the most photographed ruin in the south of England.

landmarks.name: British Airways i360
landmarks.description: A 162-metre steel pole on the seafront with a glass viewing pod that rises and falls. Designed by the team behind the London Eye. Controversial when it opened; now a fixed element of the skyline beside the West Pier ruin.

landmarks.name: Volks Electric Railway
landmarks.description: The oldest operating electric railway in the world. Runs along Madeira Drive from the Aquarium to Black Rock for 1.6 km. Original 1880s rolling stock, still in service in summer. Grade II listed.

landmarks.name: Grand Hotel
landmarks.description: The white Victorian grand hotel on the central seafront, rebuilt after the 1984 IRA bombing that attempted to kill Margaret Thatcher. Five people died; the Prime Minister survived by chance. The facade was restored to the original Whichcord design; the interior was entirely rebuilt.

cultural_refs.title: Brighton Rock (candy)
cultural_refs.creator: Edwardian-era confectioners
cultural_refs.description: The striped peppermint sugar stick with the word BRIGHTON running through it. An Edwardian confectionery trick involving hot sugar, pulling, and careful layering. A cultural export: Graham Greene's 1938 novel took its title from this candy; every UK child knows what 'a stick of Brighton Rock' means.

cultural_refs.title: Brighton Rock
cultural_refs.creator: John Boulting
cultural_refs.description: The Boulting Brothers' black-and-white adaptation with a 23-year-old Richard Attenborough as Pinkie. One of British cinema's great early noirs. Shot on location in Brighton before postwar reconstruction erased the real locations.

cultural_refs.title: Quadrophenia
cultural_refs.creator: Franc Roddam
cultural_refs.description: The film adaptation, shot on the Brighton seafront and along Madeira Drive. The Mods-vs-Rockers brawl in the Aquarium terraces is the defining set piece. Sting plays the Ace Face. The most important British youth film of the late 70s.

cultural_refs.title: Brighton Rock (remake)
cultural_refs.creator: Rowan Joffé
cultural_refs.description: The 2010 remake moves the setting from 1930s to 1964 — placing Pinkie Brown among the Mods-vs-Rockers crowd. Sam Riley as Pinkie. The period rework only makes the original's brutality clearer.

cultural_refs.title: The King's Speech
cultural_refs.creator: Tom Hooper
cultural_refs.description: The Royal Pavilion appears — standing in for its own 1930s interior — in Tom Hooper's Oscar-winner. The Pavilion was used for George VI's pre-coronation scenes.

cultural_refs.title: Mods and Rockers, Whitsun 1964
cultural_refs.creator: British youth culture
cultural_refs.description: Bank-holiday brawls along Madeira Drive between Mod scooter gangs and Rocker motorcycle gangs. Sociologist Stanley Cohen uses Brighton as his case study in the 1972 book 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics' — coining a term that now runs through decades of media criticism.

cultural_refs.title: Norman Cook (Fatboy Slim)
cultural_refs.creator: Norman Cook
cultural_refs.description: The DJ and producer behind 'The Rockafeller Skank' and 'Praise You' has lived in Brighton since the 1990s. His influence on the city's music scene — the Big Beach Boutiques, the Big Beat Boutique club nights at the Concorde 2 — is part of what made Brighton electronic music's UK capital for a decade.

cultural_refs.title: Grand Hotel bombing
cultural_refs.creator: Provisional IRA
cultural_refs.description: Patrick Magee plants a time-delay bomb in Room 629 weeks before the Conservative Party conference. It detonates at 2:54 a.m. on 12 October 1984. Margaret Thatcher is in Room 628 but is in the sitting room at the moment of the blast. She chairs the conference the next morning. Magee was jailed; released under the Good Friday Agreement in 1999.

cultural_refs.title: The Great Storm of 1987
cultural_refs.creator: Nature
cultural_refs.description: Hurricane-force winds overnight 15–16 October 1987. BBC weatherman Michael Fish had reassured viewers earlier that evening not to worry about any hurricane — one of the most famous forecasting errors in British history. The storm takes the Palace Pier's concert hall into the sea.

cultural_refs.title: Brighton Pride
cultural_refs.creator: Brighton & Hove Pride
cultural_refs.description: First held 1992. Now the UK's largest Pride event — over 400,000 across the first weekend of August. The parade starts at the seafront and ends at Preston Park, where the daytime event runs. The weekend has become the city's biggest annual occasion.

cultural_refs.title: West Pier fires
cultural_refs.creator: Unsolved
cultural_refs.description: 28 March 2003: first fire. 11 May 2003: second fire. 12 December 2004: third fire. Official investigations into the first two were inconclusive; the Crown Prosecution Service declined to prosecute. The skeleton that remains is now a protected structure.

cultural_refs.title: Brighton Rock (novel)
cultural_refs.creator: Graham Greene
cultural_refs.description: The most Catholic noir ever written. Pinkie Brown, the teenage razor-gang leader, operates under the Palace Pier. Greene stayed at the Metropole to write it. The book's title refers to the candy with the word running through it — 'the brick through which all the way it's stamped out'.

cultural_refs.title: The West Pier
cultural_refs.creator: Patrick Hamilton
cultural_refs.description: The first novel in Hamilton's Gorse trilogy, set in Brighton in the 1920s. Graham Greene called it 'the finest novel ever written about Brighton'. Underread now; still the most specifically-Brighton novel besides Greene's.

cultural_refs.title: Quadrophenia (album)
cultural_refs.creator: The Who
cultural_refs.description: Pete Townshend's double-LP rock opera about Jimmy, a disaffected London Mod whose personality splits four ways. The entire second side plays out on Brighton Beach, and ends with Jimmy riding a stolen scooter off Beachy Head. Ranks in Rolling Stone's all-time 500.

cultural_refs.title: Waterloo
cultural_refs.creator: ABBA
cultural_refs.description: Performed at the Brighton Dome on 6 April 1974, winning the Eurovision Song Contest. The performance that made ABBA global. Europop's Big Bang.

cultural_refs.title: Tom Hark
cultural_refs.creator: The Piranhas
cultural_refs.description: The ska band The Piranhas, formed in Brighton in 1978, hit the UK top ten in 1980 with a cover of 'Tom Hark'. A seaside-punk anthem that played heavily on the pier jukeboxes for the summer.

cultural_refs.title: Big Beach Boutique II
cultural_refs.creator: Fatboy Slim / Norman Cook
cultural_refs.description: A free DJ set on Brighton Beach on 13 July 2002. Expected 60,000; 250,000 came. Brighton closed to incoming rail traffic for the first time in its history. One of the largest free music events in UK history, and a Fatboy Slim career landmark.

cultural_refs.title: Royal Blood
cultural_refs.creator: Royal Blood
cultural_refs.description: The Brighton-formed two-piece — bass and drums, no guitars — hit number one with their debut album in 2014. They still live in Brighton. The bassist, Mike Kerr, went to Varndean College up the hill from the seafront.

recurring_events.name: Brighton & Hove Pride
recurring_events.description: The UK's largest Pride, 400,000+ across the weekend. The Saturday parade runs from Hove Lawns along the seafront and up Marine Parade to Preston Park; the Saturday evening concert at Preston Park draws A-list headliners. Kemptown runs its own street party on the Sunday.

recurring_events.name: The Great Escape Festival
recurring_events.description: Europe's leading new-music festival. 500+ emerging acts play 30+ venues across Brighton over three nights — pubs, churches, the Dome, the piers. Has launched the careers of Adele, Ed Sheeran, Stormzy, and most of the last decade's UK indie.

recurring_events.name: Brighton Marathon
recurring_events.description: The UK's second-biggest marathon after London. Course runs along the seafront west to Hove, east to Ovingdean, finishing at Madeira Drive. 18,000+ runners.

recurring_events.name: Brighton Festival
recurring_events.description: England's largest curated arts festival. Theatre, music, literature, visual art, spoken word across 150+ venues. Annual Guest Director brings a personal programme — recent ones: Brian Eno, Nikki Amuka-Bird, Lemn Sissay.

recurring_events.name: London to Brighton Veteran Car Run
recurring_events.description: Annual since 1896 — the world's longest-running motoring event. Pre-1905 cars drive the 60 miles from Hyde Park to Brighton seafront, arriving mid-morning. Around 400 cars finish; Madeira Drive becomes an Edwardian motor museum.

businesses.name: Regency Restaurant
businesses.description: The seafront fish restaurant since 1930. Dover sole, grilled sea bass, the oyster platter. White tablecloths, tap water in glass jugs, old-school waiters. The kind of place your grandparents took you and you take your children. Overlooks the Palace Pier.

businesses.name: English's of Brighton
businesses.description: The fish restaurant in The Lanes, serving oysters and Dover sole since 1945 in a building dating to the 1700s. Marble bar, Regency-era feel, slightly higher prices than Regency, worth it for the oysters.

businesses.name: Terre à Terre
businesses.description: Opened 1993, one of the UK's first serious vegetarian restaurants. The kind of inventive vegetarian cooking that got written about in the Guardian for a decade. Brighton's food scene measures 'before and after Terre à Terre'.

businesses.name: The Marlborough Pub & Theatre
businesses.description: A Kemptown LGBTQ+ institution with a small upstairs theatre. Drag nights, cabaret, queer theatre, 300-year-old building. The kind of place Brighton's queer cultural life happens in public view.

businesses.name: Royal Pavilion Museum
businesses.description: The museum operating George IV's 1815-22 Indo-Islamic palace — the Pavilion itself, the Saloon, the Banqueting Room, the Great Kitchen. Run by Brighton & Hove City Council. Open daily. The only building of its kind in Britain.
